summary: filesystem-permission is internally sound — clean build, C# suite 2853/2853 green, OBP shape holds, and the consent gate's creation side has no PLang-reachable fabrication path. But two issues straddle the seams the three prior reviewers each missed. F-A: persisted 'always allow' grants expire 5 minutes after creation (signing.verify applies Config.TimeoutMs's Created-age check to long-lived grants), the FileSystem/Permission/this.cs doc-comment claims permanent persistence, and Stage5 Scenario4 false-greens it because the test never advances time — the branch's headline feature does not do what it documents. Security found this (their F3) but under-rated it as a non-blocking security nit; viewed as code+tests+docs it is branch-blocking. F-B: the branch's merge-base predates the app-lowercase merge into runtime2, so it carries ~40 new files under PascalCase App/ with PascalCase namespaces that violate runtime2's current lowercase convention — no reviewer diffed against current runtime2. Verdict: FAIL, driven by F-A (which stands alone).

previous_reviews_assessed.codeanalyzer: partial — thorough OBP pass, but diagnosed the Linux case-comparison bug (Path.cs:125,127 / PLangFileSystem.cs:254) and passed without fixing or tracking it, and asserted ResumeChain dispose order 'correct' with no test behind the claim.
previous_reviews_assessed.tester: partial — all 9 v3 findings genuinely mutation-verified closed, but Scenario4 is presented as the cross-App persistence gate when it only pins the AppId-removal regression; the documented durability is unverified (test never advances NowUtc).
previous_reviews_assessed.security: partial — F1/F2/F4 well-analysed and severity-fair; F3 under-rated as non-blocking. As a correctness defect with a false doc-comment and a false-greening test, F3 is branch-blocking, not 'an improvement to land'.

findings.issue: Persisted 'always allow' grants expire 5 minutes after creation. Path.Authorize.SignAndStore calls EnsureSigned() with no Expires; VerifySignature builds `new signing.verify { Data = data }` with no TimeoutMs; Ed25519.VerifyAsync step 2 (Ed25519.cs:79-81) rejects any signature whose Created age exceeds effectiveTimeout = Config.TimeoutMs (default 300000ms). After 5 minutes TryCover returns false and the user is re-prompted. The FileSystem/Permission/this.cs:19-21 doc-comment states grants 'survive new App() on the same root, which is the contract the a answer promises' — false past 5 minutes. Stage5 Scenario4 false-greens it: the test never advances NowUtc, so it only ever reads a millisecond-old grant inside the freshness window.
findings.impact: The branch's headline feature — persistent 'always allow' consent — does not work as documented. A user who answers 'a' is re-prompted 5 minutes later. Behaviour is fail-closed (re-prompt, not bypass) so not a security hole, but it is a real functional defect in the core feature, the shipped doc-comment is false, and the test suite asserts the feature works when it does not. The team's tracked AlwaysExpiry todo addresses Expires==null only, not the independent Created-age check, so the deferred fix is insufficient.
findings.suggestion: Sign 'a' grants with an explicit long Expires AND have VerifySignature pass a TimeoutMs that disables the Created-age check for grant verification (grant's own Expires becomes the only time bound). Do NOT raise global Config.TimeoutMs — it widens the wire-message replay window. Add a test that advances NowUtc past 5 minutes. Correct the Permission/this.cs doc-comment until fixed.

findings.issue: The branch merge-base with runtime2 (79d76aa0) predates the app-lowercase merge (16ab73eb). runtime2 tip has PLang/app/ lowercase; this branch has PLang/App/ PascalCase and adds ~40 new files under it, each declaring a PascalCase namespace (namespace App.FileSystem.Permission;, namespace App.Actor.Permission;, etc.) that violates runtime2's current lowercase-namespace convention. codeanalyzer v3 rebased onto origin/filesystem-permission (the branch's own remote), not runtime2 — no reviewer diffed against the actual merge target.
findings.impact: The branch cannot merge to runtime2 as-is. Landing it requires renaming the whole new App/ subtree to app/, rewriting ~40 namespace declarations to lowercase, and updating every using / global-using alias and consumer — a substantial, convention-sensitive body of work that is currently invisible and unreviewed. 'Three PASSes' does not mean 'mergeable'.
findings.suggestion: Rebase onto current runtime2; rename PLang/App/** new files to PLang/app/**; rewrite new-file namespaces to the lowercase convention; re-run the suite; have a reviewer see the rebased diff before the branch advances to docs.

findings.issue: Path.cs:125,127 (Relative getter) and PLangFileSystem.cs:254 (system/ fallback) still use StringComparison.OrdinalIgnoreCase where the new RootComparison helper belongs. codeanalyzer v3 finding 3 diagnosed both and wrote the one-line fix, then passed the branch with them unfixed and no todos.md entry. Both confirmed still present at HEAD.
findings.impact: On Linux, a path under a mismatched-case RootDirectory yields a wrong %path.Relative% (observability, not a permission gate — security correctly excluded it as a security finding). The process issue: a diagnosed defect with a written fix was shipped without the fix or a tracking entry.
findings.suggestion: Apply RootComparison at Path.cs:125,127 and PLangFileSystem.cs:254, or file a tracked todos.md entry. Path.cs:189,194 (Equals/GetHashCode) carry the same unconditional OrdinalIgnoreCase smell — not gate-reachable, worth folding in.

findings.issue: ResumeChain's parent branch does `await using var callFrame = Push(frame.Action)` then recurses into the child, then `return await frame.Goal.RunFrom(ctx, StepIndex, ActionIndex + 1)`. The parent's post-call continuation runs INSIDE the callFrame await-using scope — the child call frame is not disposed until after the parent has fully continued, unlike normal execution where a call frame is scoped to the call action. codeanalyzer v1 asserted 'dispose order is correct' with no test behind the claim.
findings.impact: If the parent's post-resume continuation reads %!callstack%, renders an error trail, or re-suspends (capturing a fresh Snapshot), it sees/embeds a stale extra frame. SnapshotResumeTests and GoalRunFromTests pass because they assert results, not call-stack shape — the assumption is untested. Architect already flagged ResumeChain as 'clunky, revisit' in todos.md.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that asserts call-stack shape during a post-resume parent continuation (and a re-suspend after resume), or restructure so the call frame disposes before the parent advances past the call action.

findings.issue: MoveCopyBundledConsentTests exercises bundled (single-prompt) consent on Path.MoveTo/CopyTo (v2 surface). The action handlers modules/file/copy.cs and move.cs — the path a real PLang copy/move step takes — issue two sequential prompts (documented v1 degradation). Bundled consent is tested only on a surface PLang programs do not reach.
findings.impact: Not a defect (the degradation is documented in the handler doc-comments), but the test name implies coverage of a UX that shipped code paths do not deliver.
findings.suggestion: Either note in the test that it covers only the v2 surface, or add a handler-path test asserting the two-prompt behaviour so the gap is explicit.
